question: Expertise in the Recommendation, Planning, and Implementation of Customized Websites
answer:
All our sites are a custom plan and a custom build. While we do have a library of proven strategies, starter messages to send to stakeholders, accessibility tested features, and a fairly standard process plan, all projects and sites are custom and unique.

The criteria that we use when crafting our recommendations include influences such as:

Team size and availability

Timeline

Amount of content

Integrations needed

Current platform stability or expiring license

Scope and number of sites merging together

While custom, there are set UX rules that exist. Users expect certain things certain ways. They expect underlined text to be a link, they look for the site search in the top right, as well as certain interactions and behaviors. Our sites must follow these learned behaviors—your site should not be a site where they must learn how to use it or that is different from the majority of other sites they visit.

We also have a data and research team. Even with unique clients, there are some things that are more universal in how people interact and engage with websites. This data brings a strong foundation for your site.

A few custom site examples:

Roswell Park Comprehensive Cancer Center: Part of the site was already launched, and our project was to integrate all of Research and Education (from other sites) to make one site all while keeping the current site live.

University of Florida: A very decentralized content model with over 200 sites, our first phase was to create a new top-level site and then create a flexible platform that all the other sites could adapt if they choose to on their own unique CMS.

Galen College of Nursing: With over 26 locations, each location needed localized SEO while also functioning as a national presence.

question: Site Implementation Questions - WordPress
answer:
1. If your theme or plugin uses templates to place content, describe the steps in creating a template.Stamats builds on the base WordPress core for building additional templates. Our flexible layout allows all components to be used on any pages making it simple to create patterns for reuse on pages without the need for additional templates.

Additional templates or page types can be created by copying existing or creating new page PHP files. This allows content authors to easily select components when managing pages.

2. Describe the skill set needed to create or maintain these templates.Reusable patterns can be created by content authors. Used when the style and layout are not different, but reusing existing components.

New page types or templates when a new design or layout is needed uses PHP, HTML, and CSS experience. Once built, these are easily usable by content authors.3. Describe how your product applies or enforces style restrictions and constraints on text or graphic content. For example, if the text content is longer than the area allotted for placement, or the graphic content is smaller or larger than the allotted space.Custom blocks are created based on the design. Flexible areas are allowed to expand. Restricted areas are sized to a specific display area.

Text fields can be restricted by character count. Stamats uses the default WordPress core and Advanced Custom Fields. Allows for custom development for additional restrictions.

Images will automatically resize based on the space of the area, maintaining the same aspect ratio to prevent distortion. For clearer images, they should be resized from large to small size.

4. Does your theme or plugin support templates that allow content to be dynamically queried from the RunTime server? Explain in detail how this is accomplished and how non-technical users can create these queries on their pages.

Yes, dynamic content and queries can be added using the Stamats build. The flexibility exists to create API connections and queries to other data sources.

First the query or connection should be created in development—then made available to non-technical users to add to pages and any configuration options when applicable.

Existing code snippets or embeds can also easily be placed by non-technical users.

5. Does your theme or plugin allow the bulk import of existing web pages and assets into the CMS?Yes, a plugin or custom import you choose to use is supported. These will support migrations where the content and fields are very similar.

When moving to a new design, a one-to-one relationship in content may not exist. An automated mapping process would need to be incorporated into your import process. A new design is an opportunity to restructure, optimize, and write new content which is better supported by a manual migration.

WordPress supports bulk upload of assets.

question: Connected User Journey
answer:
As we develop the strategy, our goal is to ensure that the natural conversations during a tour are expressed online. If during a tour, the guide would comment about the facilities, available technology, or the ways faculty/doctors/nurses connect with their students/patients, then the online experience should do the same. This interconnective experience turns previously static tours into interactive experiences that mimic the emotion and feel of a personal visit.

During the storyboarding process, we will consider:

What are the emotional needs of the visitor?

What information do they need to make the next decision towards conversion?

How would they consume this content in person?

What supporting experiences would they have?

What questions would the visitor have at each point?

question: Photography/Videography
answer:
Stamats partners with top industry photographers and can help [Client Name] select the right fit, whether nationally recognized or local talent. Once we understand your asset needs, we'll recommend the best combination to meet your goals.

Contracts are made directly with the photographer, and you retain full rights to all images and files.

Our designers bring strong photo direction skills and will support your team before, during, and after the shoot. We'll work collaboratively to identify subjects and ensure the shoot is productive, on brand, and aligned with your strategy.

Stamats and our preferred partners can provide photography services related to:

Strategy, planning, and scheduling – Your Stamats’ designer will share a Preparing for the Photo Shoot guide and work with the UTSW team and the photographer to make a one-, two-, or three-day shoot as efficient and productive as possible.

Editing – Stamats’ partner photographers provide full-service photography services, including editing. Stamats’ designers are also adept at editing for use in creative assets of all types.

Aerial/drone – If desired, our photography partners can also capture drone footage as part of a directed photo shoot.

Metadata/keyword tagging – Stamats will learn your taxonomy and tagging system and create a process for properly tagging new assets created from our shoots.

For marquee events, we will schedule photo shoot preparation, the shoot itself, and post-production activities as we would any other important project. The schedule will include timelines, responsibilities, due dates, and deliverables.

Adherence to media release procedures – Our responsibility is to you, our client. We follow your policies and procedures.

For campaign-specific work, we often spend three days on site to create (or complement) a photography and video asset library that can be used across all communications. A shoot of this length typically yields 100-200 final shots and B-roll video.

However, sometimes clients (especially those with large or dispersed teams) require longer review windows at various points than the timeline anticipated. question: Typical Timeline for Digital Advertising Initiatives
answer:
Stamats, within 60 days of contract start date, in collaboration with [Client Name]staff, will develop a strategic digital plan with a timeline of all programs, activities, and deadlines to create and implement campaigns.

Month 1

Day 1 Kickoff meeting between Stamats and the [Client Name]team. This meeting is when the timeline clock starts.

The Stamats Campaign Execution Plan document is reviewed, and the client has one week to provide needed information.

The launch phase timeline is reviewed.

Launch and weekly meeting schedule and attendees discussed with the client.

Week 2 Once the completed Execution Plans are returned to Stamats, campaign structures are created in the appropriate platforms and keyword research begins.

Concurrently, necessary contributions to the Strategic Brief are provided to the Stamats project manager.

Landing page and Display ad creation begin.

Week 3 The first draft of ad and landing page copy is provided to the client for approval.

Search text ads.

Including search ad extensions: callouts, site links, phone numbers, structured data.

Banner image ads.

Social media ads.

Landing page copy.

Note: The client has three working days to review, process approvals, and suggest changes. This does not need to be an exhaustive review. This step in the process is to find inaccuracies or branding problems. Ad copy can be updated at any time while the campaigns are in-flight.

Month 2

Week 1 Final ad and landing page copy is distributed to the client.

Week 2 All campaigns are prepped for launch. Landing page(s) are completed.

Week 3 All campaigns launch.

Week 4 Final launch phase meeting, review of outstanding tasks. Client-facing dashboard is released and reviewed during the meeting.

Months 3 through 12

Stamats begins daily/weekly/monthly optimization cadence.

Ad copy is adjusted or updated as needed. The goal is one hero and two challenger assets in each ad group.

This step of the process takes high performing parts from challenger ads and combines them into new ads.

New ads may be written with slight variations learned from actual consumer search queries.

For example, if the ads use the term 'business program' but consumers consistently search for ‘business degrees.’

A monthly performance reporting meeting each month.

This report meeting can be no sooner than seven days after the reporting month.

Each month will provide month-over-month performance comparisons and flight-to-date performance metrics. This part of the meeting should take less than 20 minutes.

The balance of the reporting meeting will be used to:

Discuss any new observations on prospective student behaviors.

Sharing proposed experiments for the campaigns, testing to improve performance.

Updates from the client on lead quality or application/registration performance.

Changes in competitor behaviors.

Insights to share from other related marketing channels.

question: Media Buying Scope of Work Approach (SOW and Implementation Plan)
answer:

question: Usability Testing (Optional)
answer:
Describe how you will test the development work with end-users.

A unique feature for Stamats clients is our integrated approach to research and the user experience. As a key part of our process, we can test navigation and proposed concepts through usability tools, crowdsourcing, and other methodologies based on key questions and tasks. Our plans assess overall impressions, user-experience factors, navigation experience, features that inspire engagement, and journey path. Based on findings, we recommend revisions to resolve and address areas for improvement.

Usability testing grounds our website design, architecture, and theory in reality—the reality of users and their experiences with the site. We test for intuitiveness, navigability, and user journeys. We collect both qualitative and quantitative data for various user tasks, giving us evidence to shape the new architecture, wireframes, and design.

Usability testing can take the shape needed and adapts to different phases of the redesign process. As part of discovery, it helps us identify strengths and weaknesses in an existing site. As part of design, it helps us adjust the architecture, interface, and content. After launch, it strengthens content and guides implementation.

We will work with [Client Name] to define the best use of an overall usability testing budget (which is built into the contract). We determine which type of testing is most appropriate for which audience, for which tasks, and at what stage(s) of the redesign process.

What will be the deliverable?

Usability strategy plan, usability test, recruitment strategy, execution of the usability test, report and recommendations, and raw data.

What will be the development test plan deliverable?

Testing to the requirements, accessibility testing, video recordings of testing, bug tracker, and validation. We provide a summary of testing results.

Myriad Testing Options for Sitewide Improvements

We can incorporate qualitative and quantitative testing throughout the redesign, including options such as:

Heuristic Review​

User Journey Audits​

Heat Mapping​

Video Evaluation ​

Tree Testing​

First Click Test

Label Testing ​

Card Sort Test​

Together we determine which type of testing is most appropriate for which audience, for which tasks, and at what stage(s) of the project.

question: Print Was Where It All Began for Stamats
answer:
Starting in the 1950s, Stamats was a leading rapid-press publisher of quality print collateral for colleges and universities. In the 1960s, seeing a need for creative services, Stamats began designing and writing dozens of Viewbooks, which became our main service for over a decade.

As a full-service agency, our research and audience intelligence teams provide insights into the latest market trends that savvy institutions are using in creative communications. Over the course of the ensuing 60 years, Stamats has been recognized as the premier company in producing distinctive, strategic print publications for colleges and universities.

We have developed publications for public institutions such as the University of Michigan and University of Kentucky and private institutions such as Harvard and Wagner (NYC). Our work has won scores of awards from CASE and AMA. Our range of work includes:

Large and small viewbooks and campaign pieces, with perfect binding and special effects such as foil stamping, embossing, and glued pockets; high impact, large fold-out brochures; smaller mailers; folders, postcards, and packets.

Creative personalized campaigns. Stamats works with preferred vendors and other printing companies for fulfilment of personalized, print on demand campaigns.

Strategic planning for publications that determines each point of contact in the admissions funnel, the medium, the message, and the call-to-action.

Integration of print with digital and web-based campaigns, including flexible print and ad templates for use on an ongoing and as-needed basis

In addition to full production, we can develop flexible print and ad templates for [Client Name]'s use on an ongoing and as-needed basis.

question: Storytelling and Content Creation
answer:
The Stamats content framework keeps strategy front and center, aligning each page with clear goals, audience intent, and calls-to-action. No matter how users arrive—search, social, email, or referral—we focus on creating positive, purposeful experiences.

We’ll begin by assessing your current content landscape:

What’s working, and what isn’t?

Where are the gaps?

What are your goals and audience expectations?

With that insight, we’ll partner with your team to build content that drives measurable impact—boosting pageviews, reducing bounce rates, increasing conversions, and growing your audience.

Our proven approach to content marketing has helped clients gain millions of pageviews, improve search visibility, and turn engagement into institutional results.

Your content and digital strategists will review your on-page user experience data and provide focused, proactive ideas to improve your content, grow your audiences, and increase engagement.

Deliverable: Kick-off strategy meeting with QuickStart Guide.

Subject Matter Expert Interviews

Stamats content strategists are trained in journalism best practices; we will not ask your subject matter experts (SMEs) basic questions. We value their time and will focus our interviews on their expert opinions and your differentiators.

In collaboration with your team, we will schedule interviews with your SMEs via email. We will prepare your experts by concisely conveying the goal/topic of the interview, their (minimal!) time investment, the format, and the approval process. We typically conduct interviews by telephone, Zoom, or Microsoft Teams.

Ghostwriting & Podcast Scripting

When we develop content, we make sure every story is focused on your goals and the needs of your audience. Our content strategists understand how people read on the web and use internet search tools to find relevant content; we write to these requirements to improve the user experience and increase your search rankings.

Stamats content strategists are experts in capturing the personal and institutional brand voices that make your SMEs and institution unique. We will infuse your compelling stories with their originality, as well as keywords that will help readers find your content online.

All stories are created with audience-focused digital best practices and relevant, streamlined user experiences with the goal of conversion. Stamats tailors your content to your unique goals and opportunities:

Conversion-centered content best practices

Audience-specific engagements

Thoughtful phrasing for audience relevance and digital storytelling

Drive further engagement with inline links and callouts, reducing bounce rates

Strategic call-to-action (CTA) strategies

Guidance on next steps for continued engagement (the “and then”)

Simplified conversion pathways

Posts for two social channels, such as Facebook and LinkedIn (or two of your choice)

Digital best practices

Mobile-first content creation

Scannability, including headings and proper page structure

Search engine optimization (SEO), including keyword usage and placement

Technical content elements

Alt text

Meta descriptions

URL recommendations

Photo captions

Email Development

Stamats has crafted content for hundreds of enrollment marketing campaigns for institutions of all sizes across the U.S. We understand the nuances of email marketing to prospective students and families. Audience needs are at the core of successful messaging: What do they want and need to know about Iowa to make a decision?

Working closely with your team, we will uncover key information to guide the email creation process, including:

Audience characteristics (e.g., undergrad/year in high school, transfers, grad students, and others)

The clear, simple message each email should convey

Next steps, such as deadlines, contact information, and what’s next

Design suggestions to properly balance images/video, text, and calls-to-action

Landing pages and are we continuing the discussion and steps leading to matriculation

Right Messaging, Right Time

Working collaboratively with the Iowa team, we will match the intent for each message with the intended audience and next step. From there, our enrollment marketing content specialists will create compelling, actionable content for your email sends.

Content Management

Content Status Calls

Up front, our team provides detailed timelines that clearly and accurately represent each task in the project, who is responsible for completing each task (including members of your project team), what deliverables are required, and what key dates and milestones need to be met. Throughout the engagement, we conduct internal status meetings and provide regular project status updates with your team.

Deliverable: Weekly Zoom or Microsoft Teams calls through the duration of the project.

Editorial Calendar

Creating and managing a centralized calendar helps your team and Stamats stay on track. As part of your scope of work, Stamats will update and manage an integrated blog and podcast editorial calendar based on your business goals and currently scheduled content.

Deliverable: Editorial calendar in Google Sheets or Microsoft Excel OneDrive.

Workshops & Trainings

Email and eNewsletter Writing Best Practices

Email is highly popular and highly effective—and will become increasingly important in the world of cookieless digital advertising. It’s one of the most underrated distribution channels, and it’s a natural route to deliver unique, relevant news and stories directly to your digital audience.​

Email keeps you at the top of your audience’s mind. ​Recipients can share your content within their personal networks, offering organic audience growth.​ In this comprehensive workshop, our certified professional email team will discuss content structure, conversion strategies, accessibility, compliance, and readability.

If you choose this session, you’ll walk away with ideas to streamline your email messaging and increase your return on investment.

SEO 101

This workshop will help you learn the basics: What search engine optimization (SEO) is, why it is important, and how to implement best practices. Upon completion, you will have the groundwork to create user-centric content that ranks higher in Google search and attracts relevant engagements.

Tips our SEO experts cover in this training session include:

Google’s Helpful Content Update: Guiding Users to information they seek

Appropriately adding relevant search phrases to your content

Optimizing your content for search engines and for users

Using links from other relevant websites in your content

Monitoring and measuring your search rankings and results

Web & Social Media Storytelling 101

Storytelling is so much more than words on a page. In our storytelling workshop, we teach you to find your stories with a defined context, clear audience, focused purpose, on-brand keywords, and unique angle. Once you get comfortable with doing this, your team can replicate this process in-house, on repeat.

Our expert digital storytellers can teach you the ins and outs of:

Understanding the elements of what makes a “good story”

Writing compelling headlines and subheadings

Knowing where to find stories and who should “tell” them

Coming up with timely and evergreen content topics

Understanding differences between news and consumer stories—and how to cross over

Creating effective calls-to-action (CTAs)

Implementing streamlined story distribution strategies on social media

Pairing visuals and video with text to create compelling posts

Updating content over time (content governance)

question: User Experience Assessment/Journey Mapping
answer:
A well-mapped user journey is key to a strong user experience. Stamats audits the full path—from Google search or referral site through to conversion—identifying pain points and missed opportunities along the way.

Our team conducts mystery shopping on desktop and mobile, evaluating three user journeys to uncover barriers, improve engagement, and strengthen conversion paths. We focus on:

Consideration: What brings users in

Clarification: What questions they ask

Conversion: Where the journey ends and action begins

Each journey is mapped with clear recommendations. The process includes:

Two prep meetings to select journeys and gather data (e.g., analytics, heatmaps)

Contextual evaluation aligned with your information architecture

Heatmap placement and GA view-only access

Up to three presentation sessions (one 90-min or three 30-min meetings)

Deliverable: A PowerPoint report and guided presentation of findings to the [Client Name]team.

Cost: Three journeys is $10,800. Each additional journey is $3,500.

Additional Content Services: Based on the audit findings, Stamats can rewrite pages in the journeys as well as SEO optimization. Depending on the level of editing required, the price per page ranges from $XXX to $XXX.

question: Approach to Adjusting Content for an Audience
answer:
Stamats strategists excel at turning complex data into clear, tailored visual stories for both lay and peer audiences. With just one interview, our team can create multiple content pieces for different platforms and audiences—thanks to decades of healthcare content expertise and smart, targeted questioning.

For example, a story on the .edu site may use higher level vocabulary and be slanted more toward researchers, faculty, and trainees, whereas a story on same topic for the .org site likely will be tweaked with lay-friendly language and calls-to-action for prospective patients and referring physicians.

Stamats follows the Create Once, Publish Everywhere (COPE) model, which is the ultimate guide to “upcycling” content. We understand that enterprise storytelling requires equal parts creativity and efficiency! As such, we are proficient in setting up interviews and subsequent content to serve different audience perspectives and fit the expected length and digestibility of the platform on which the content will be served.

question: Key Challenges and Opportunities for this Project
answer:
Without the benefit of our comprehensive Discovery, at this point we can see a few potential challenges/opportunities associated with this project.

[FOR A CLIENT THAT PLANS TO LAUNCH IN STAGES/PHASES] Launch Strategy. If the site is launched in phases, there will be connections or duplicate content that might not yet be updated (e.g., coordination and alignment so that one site doesn’t hold back the others). Attentive management during the transition will be essential. We will address this issue with a comprehensive plan, a content strategy, and clarity on what content lives where (internet versus intranet versus extranet), a clear internal communication plan, and a “gap strategy.” If the site is launched all together in Phase I, it will be important that one site delay does not impact the overall launch. Clear timelines, responsibility trackers, and a communication plan will be critical throughout the project.

Mobile Design. The current site relies heavily on left navigation, which research shows performs poorly on mobile compared to inline links. While Stamats will deliver a highly functioning mobile experience, the content will need to be updated to create pathways within the page.

Lack of Marketing Message on Program pages. From an outside view, the site is missing a clear message about great programs and educational experience. They cover the facts of the programs and lack that powerful why. If a prospective student asked you about your pilot program, it is unlikely that you would read the content on the website. Instead, you would most likely describe the excellent program and what makes it special at Client Name. The website is often the first point of evaluation and therefore must be a key part of your marketing plan.

[IF CLIENT HAS A LOT OF PDFS] PDF Reliance. The site has nearly XXXX PDFs which is not an ideal way to present content. PDFs often do not pass accessibility, putting you at risk. While your RFP did not address PDFs, this will be a critical part of your content and accessibility strategy.

Large Content Contributor Team. With XXXX content contributors, we can assume there is an ongoing challenge of keeping the team updated in content and accessibility requirements. Our reusable asset strategy and content templates are designed specifically to help institutions with hundreds of users provide a consistent experience. This inconsistency can impact your site performance.

question: Accessible Social Media Content
answer:
About 25% of U.S. adults have a disability—it’s imperative that social content follows accessibility standards. Our team invests in training every week to stay on top of social media trends and accessibility best practices, such as:

Including alt text on images whenever possible

Providing descriptive content in the text areas of image-heavy posts

Adhering to appropriate color contrast standards for text-on-image posts

Using #TitleCase on hashtags

Providing captions and transcriptions for videos, regardless of platform

We’ll keep an eye out for opportunities to improve your social content navigability and usability. Our team spans a range of genders, backgrounds, and ages (five decades, in fact!), bringing holistic, experience-led perspectives to every content marketing project.

question: American Standard Case Study
answer:
For the Love of Home

American Standard was suffering from an identity crisis. The iconic brand had lost its luster. Research uncovered that while the brand enjoyed high awareness, it lacked appeal and desire among a new generation of consumers who viewed the brand as boring, basic, and (worst yet) “a brand for my parents or grandparents.”

Challenge

Despite a host of purposeful innovations and a collection of beautifully styled products, most people viewed American Standard as making just basic white toilets. Our job was to redefine American Standard for a new generation of homeowners who were seeking brands that not only worked great, but also fit perfectly into their not-so-ordinary lives.

Solution

Through extensive research and interviews with internal and external audiences, we developed a consumer-centric brand platform that honored American Standard’s 150-year legacy while speaking relevantly and emotionally to a younger, family-focused, female audience that values the authenticity, dependability, and practicality American Standard brings to the heart of the home.

As a result, we developed a persona-based marketing program that animated the brand across her purchase journey.

Results

Our work included brand foundation, brand expression, brand guidelines, copywriting, collateral, digital, media planning, print, radio, social media, video, out of home, and web design.

Extensive campaign testing among the target audience confirmed that our approach was spot on. Brand perception, desire, and likelihood to purchase all increased as a result of exposing consumers to the campaign.

The brand platform serves as the centerpiece of an internal reinvention to turn the company from a manufacturing-focused to a consumer-centric organization.

question: Admissions Communication Sequence Mapping

question: Experience Working with Cascade
answer:
While we’re CMS-agnostic, we specialize in three systems—Cascade, Modern Campus CMS, and WordPress—focusing on personalization, content sharing, and governance. Our team has deep expertise in HTML and CSS for Cascade, working with Hannon Hill for review and final implementation.

Our Business Analysts and Information Architects have extensive experience designing shared content blocks and libraries. Over the years, we’ve placed thousands of Cascade pages, driven by our content strategy, which defines five types of content:

Unique Content: Non-dynamic, page-level content.

Prefilled Content: Pre-filled content blocks to streamline setup.

Shared Content: Global content blocks edited and maintained at the global level.

Taxonomy Driven: Auto-populated content based on matching tags.

Personalized Content: Dynamic content based on user behavior.

Having our developers complete Cascade training ensures smoother handoffs and improves overall development. We’ve been asked to step in on projects where previous partners lacked taxonomy understanding or failed to use reusable content blocks, causing significant post-launch issues.

Our approach integrates technology, design, and content to enhance user experience. We’ve also developed key integrations, including:

Single sign-on

Doctor modules with back-end integration

Personalization and engagement strategies

Program finders and location tools

Appointment form and event calendar integrations

question: Difficulties Anticipated - Content Projects
answer:
In content creation, jumping into trending conversations can be just as impactful as publishing evergreen stories. Yet subject matter experts often wait for all the details—missing valuable engagement windows.

Digital content is meant to evolve. We recommend sharing timely, on-brand insights early, then updating as new information emerges. For example, after Prince’s sudden death in 2016, an online conversation about opioid use took off—well before the cause was confirmed.

Two clients—Edward-Elmhurst Health (EEH) and Providence Health—considered covering Prince’s sudden death on their blogs. EEH responded within 24 hours, noting the cause was unconfirmed but using the moment to discuss substance use disorder treatment. Providence waited for full details before publishing.

The results were clear: EEH gained SEO traction, engaged readers, and drove appointments. Providence saw limited impact.

The same applies to research news—you don’t need to wait for final results to build interest. We’d collaborate with your marketing team to quickly gather content and expedite reviews to keep pace with the conversation.

question: What technologies do you think will emerge within the next 2-3 years?
answer:
REVIEW AT EACH USAGE FOR TIMELY/RELEVANCE.

AI & Machine LearningAs AI use grows alongside demand for privacy, marketers must balance automation with control. Tools like Google’s Performance Max unify campaigns across platforms, requiring a shift toward integrated, data-driven strategies—or risk reduced effectiveness.

Voice SearchWith the rise of Alexa and similar devices, voice-enabled search and social engagement are reshaping marketing. Content—from websites to ads—must be optimized for audio playback and multitasking environments.

Privacy & SecurityEvolving privacy policies, like those in Apple’s Safari, continue to limit data access. Marketers must adapt to reduced tracking capabilities and rely more on consent-driven, first-party data strategies.

System ConnectionsIntegrations like SFTP and CRM-email platform syncing will play a bigger role in automating data flow and improving communication without manual processes.

Internet of Things (IoT)As devices connect in new ways, including through VR/AR and the Metaverse, marketers must consider not just what’s connected—but how people interact within those spaces. Smart campuses exemplify this, offering seamless, personalized experiences.

Headless CMSHeadless CMS platforms allow content to be updated once and deployed everywhere, supporting consistency and agility across all digital channels.

ChatbotsWith third-party cookies fading, chatbots are rising in importance. They deliver personalized user experiences, capture first-party data, and help maintain engagement beyond the website visit.

question: How has your company responded to the demise of third-party cookies?
answer:
The end of third-party cookies is imminent. Safari and Firefox blocked them in 2019, and Chrome is expected to follow by 2024. Today, third-party cookies only reach about 45% of site traffic. As the shift to privacy-first tracking continues, Google is also replacing Universal Analytics with GA4—designed from the ground up for first-party data collection.

Stamats has long advised clients to adopt GA4, not just to prepare for the sunset of UA, but because GA4 is a more powerful tool for collecting meaningful, privacy-safe insights. While first-party data won’t fully replace third-party cookies, it creates new, ethical opportunities for audience understanding and engagement.

Our team is Google-certified in Analytics and Tag Manager, with deep experience building custom GA4 setups, dashboards (Looker Studio and Domo), and running training sessions on KPIs, event tracking, and conversion measurement.

And if we’re being bold:Websites don’t act—people do. Our job is to track what matters, report real user behavior, and guide your strategy for stronger digital performance.

question: Accessibility policy
answer:
Accessibility Statement for Stamats

Stamats is committed to ensuring digital accessibility for people with disabilities. We are continually improving the user experience for everyone and applying the relevant accessibility standards.

Conformance Status

Stamats follows WCAG 2.2 Level AA guidelines to improve accessibility. External resources like videos, PDFs, or CMS systems may have limitations beyond our control.

Technical Specifications

Stamats accessibility relies on the following technologies to work with web browsers and any assistive technologies or installed plugins:

HTML

WAI-ARIA

CSS

JavaScript

These technologies are relied upon for conformance with the accessibility standards used.

Assessment Approach

Stamats includes the following approaches for evaluation of WCAG 2.2 AA requirements:

Manual and visual evaluation

Automated technologies using WAVE evaluation tool

Automated technologies reports may be provided by client

Screen Reader using NVDA

Feedback

We welcome feedback on accessibility. If you encounter issues or have suggestions, we will work with you to address them.

question: Accessibility Review Checklist
answer:
Wave Accessibility Evaluation Tool (WAVE)

WCAG 2.2 failures are detected using the automated WAVE tool including:

Link issues

Low contrast

Unlabeled elements

Heading structure

ARIA attributes

HTML semantics

CSS defines styles

Images have alt attributes

Skip links provided

Frames and iFrames must be formatted for screen readers to understand Forms:

Instructions for the whole form are included before the <form> element

Placeholder text is not used to provide labels (ideal, not required)

Use native form controls and labels if possible

Keyboard control is tested

Form controls have an accessible name

Source order are logical

Required fields are clearly marked

Form input is validated

Nu HTML Checker: Provides review of HTML including checks for compliance with WCAG 2.2 standards.

Content Review

Pre-recorded audio includes a transcript

Content is in a meaningful sequence

No loss of text when using text resizer

Text is not represented as an image

Esc key removes content focus or hover

Provide a way to pause auto scrolling content

Each page has a page title

Links stand on their own. Links have a purpose

Font should be clear and easy to read

Navigation should identify the page you are on

Color Contrast Analyzer

Overlay to verify that color contrast on page for various WCAG contrast levels. Standard level of evaluation is AA.

Links are easily identifiable through non-color means

Keyboard:

Use keyboard to access all content on the site including all nav items, rotators, links in the expected order.

Focus state is evaluated to ensure that all interactive elements use a visual indicator when the element has keyboard focus

All functionality is available via keyboard
